summary: Jeff Atwood explores how to create enum values using C# keywords as names via escaped identifiers (@ prefix in C#, brackets in VB.NET). After researching the solution, he questions whether answering 'how' misses the more important question of 'why.' He uses the classic doctor joke to argue that just because you can work around language constraints doesn't mean you should—compiler resistance often signals a code smell.
key_points: Escaped identifiers allow using reserved keywords as names: @ prefix in C#, brackets in VB.NET
The C# implementation is awkward—you must use the @ prefix everywhere, though the actual value remains clean
Knowing how to do something doesn't mean you should do it; the 'why' question matters more
Compiler restrictions often exist for good reasons and fighting them usually indicates a design problem
Cleverness in working around language constraints can lead to absurd, unmaintainable code
money_quotes: How do I create an enum with a name that happens to be a C# keyword?
After spending 30 minutes researching the answer and playing with the results, I began to wonder if the real answer to this question should be another question: why do you need to do this?
At some point it all becomes a little ridiculous. What's next – an enum named 'enum?' A variable named 'variable?'
A man goes to a doctor's office. He says, 'Doctor, it hurts when I raise my arm over my head.' The doctor replies, 'Then don't raise your arm over your head.'
If the compiler is telling you it hurts when you do something, maybe you should stop doing it.
Just something to consider before merrily swimming your way upstream.
key_insight: When you find yourself fighting the compiler or language design, pause to ask whether you're solving the wrong problem rather than celebrating your clever workaround.
